coredata.dc:title: ROS-responsive mechanically and electronically controllable conductive hydrogel sensor with NIR modulated photothermal therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Jo, Hyeong Jun
Im, Gwang-Bum
Robby, Akhmad Irhas
In, Insik
Bhang, Suk Ho
Shit, Arnab
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: This study illustrates the design of a cancer microenvironment-selective detection system via the monitoring of wireless pressure and strain sensing response from a reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive carbon dot (CD)-embedded conductive hydrogel. ROS scavenging activity and photothermal therapy (PTT) were employed to eliminate the tumor condition. The ROS-responsive hydrogel (dsCD-Hydrogel) contained diselenide-crosslinked CD, which was dismantled in the cancer microenvironment. After being free from aggregation, the fluorescence intensity, charge carrier density, and hydrogen bonding interactions were increased. As a result, the conductivity, mechanical stretchability, and strain-pressure responses were enhanced by 52%, 122%, and 91%, respectively. The dsCD-Hydrogel produced distinct electronic signals in the presence of cancer cells according to the higher strain–pressure sensitivity in the handwriting experiment compared to normal cells. Ex-situ measurements also revealed an excellent pressure-sensing response in tumor-carrying mice. The ROS scavenging activity of the dsCD-Hydrogel decreased tumor volume. Furthermore, NIR irradiation via PTT abolished the tumor, which was verified by the downregulation of tumor hypoxia by vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF-1α) expression. To ease the monitoring procedure and acquire real-time conductivity and pressure profiles on cellphones, a wireless sensor device was deployed. Overall, a theragnostic sensor based on strain–pressure hydrogel that exhibits direct anticancer activity via PTT was successfully constructed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
ROS-responsive hydrogel
Photothermal therapy
Pressure sensing, cancer sensing
Wireless sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the dsCD-Hydrogel with the change in conductivity for tumor detection. (b) PL spectra of the dsCD with H2O2 treatment. (c) Confocal microscopic image and (d) SEM image of the dsCD-Hydrogel before and after 12 h of H2O2 treatment (scale bar 10 µm). (e) DPPH inhibition of the dsCD-Hydrogel according to time (Inset: optical images of the assay solution). (f) Tensile property of the dsCD-Hydrogel. (g) Strain sweep measurements of the dsCD-Hydrogel at a constant frequency of 1 Hz. (h) Dynamic rheological test data obtained by cyclically varying the strain before and after H2O2 treatment.

(a) Nyquist plots of the dsCD-Hydrogel before and after H2O2 treatment. (b) Wireless resistance of the dsCD-Hydrogel before and after H2O2 treatment (below: the power of LED light depends on resistance). (c) Change in resistance as a function of pressure and linear fitting curve. (d) Cyclic response of pressure with different loadings. (e) Cyclic stability of the finger bending motion before and after H2O2 treatment. (f) Cyclic response of the strain with different loadings. (g) Radial pulse motion response of the dsCD-Hydrogel. Handwriting character response of the dsCD-Hydrogel (h) before and (i) after H2O2 treatment.

(a) CLSM image of cellular uptake by HeLa and MDCK and their corresponding depth profile. (b) Flow cytometry analysis of cells (the control is black). (c) Nyquist plots of the dsCD-Hydrogel after MDCK and HeLa cell treatment. (d) Cyclic response of pressure with different loadings. Handwriting character response of dsCD-Hydrogel with (e) MDCK and (f) HeLa treatment. (g) Determination of ROS scavenging by the H2O2 assay test. FACS analysis for cell counting, and live and dead cell assay for detached HeLa cells (h) before and (i) after NIR irradiation. (j) Live and dead cell assay using CLSM imaging of HeLa cells after detachment from the surface using visible light (scale bar 100 µm).

(a) Schematic representation of hydrogel implantation on the tumor-bearing mice followed by photothermal treatment and the ex-situ wireless conductivity data. (b) The photothermal temperature increase of the implanted hydrogel. (c) Comparison of the tumor size after 3 days of implantation of the hydrogel. (d) Relative gene expression of VEGF and HIF-1α. (e) ROS concentration monitoring using CLSM. Evaluation of the (f) Source meter resistance, (g) Cyclic response of pressure with different loadings, and (h) wireless pressure sensing of the different implanted hydrogels according to their implantation conditions on the tumor-bearing mouse. PVA/Alg-Hydrogel contains only the pristine network of PVA, and Sodium alginate is used as a control to evaluate the effect of dsCD.

head.ce:title: ROS-responsive mechanically and electronically controllable conductive hydrogel sensor with NIR modulated photothermal therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study illustrates the design of a cancer microenvironment-selective detection system via the monitoring of wireless pressure and strain sensing response from a reactive oxygen species (ROS)-responsive carbon dot (CD)-embedded conductive hydrogel. ROS scavenging activity and photothermal therapy (PTT) were employed to eliminate the tumor condition. The ROS-responsive hydrogel (dsCD-Hydrogel) contained diselenide-crosslinked CD, which was dismantled in the cancer microenvironment. After being free from aggregation, the fluorescence intensity, charge carrier density, and hydrogen bonding interactions were increased. As a result, the conductivity, mechanical stretchability, and strain-pressure responses were enhanced by 52%, 122%, and 91%, respectively. The dsCD-Hydrogel produced distinct electronic signals in the presence of cancer cells according to the higher strain–pressure sensitivity in the handwriting experiment compared to normal cells. Ex-situ measurements also revealed an excellent pressure-sensing response in tumor-carrying mice. The ROS scavenging activity of the dsCD-Hydrogel decreased tumor volume. Furthermore, NIR irradiation via PTT abolished the tumor, which was verified by the downregulation of tumor hypoxia by vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF-1α) expression. To ease the monitoring procedure and acquire real-time conductivity and pressure profiles on cellphones, a wireless sensor device was deployed. Overall, a theragnostic sensor based on strain–pressure hydrogel that exhibits direct anticancer activity via PTT was successfully constructed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a severe hazard to human health since it has a high risk of incidence and fatality. Diagnosis at an early stage and the prevention of cancer have become primary global targets [1]. Although surgery is the primary line of treatment for tumor excision, surgeons cannot ensure that all malignant tissues are detected and removed [2]. Traditional cancer cell detection technologies based on tumor markers, such as immunosensors and immunoassays, are unsuitable for clinical use since measurements are often done in clinical labs utilizing sophisticated techniques like atomic spectrometry, ion-selective potentiometry, and colorimetric assays [3–5]. Further, there is an undesirable time gap between the collection of body fluid and recording of results; such delay is not acceptable, specifically for ICU patients whose situations necessitate real-time monitoring [6]. Quick-responding chemical sensors for point of care testing (POCT) enables to acquire test results without the use of centralized or complex analytical instruments [7,8]. Accordingly, a portable sensing method that can selectively and accurately detect the cancer environment via a handheld test could be developed to obtain rapid and specific results without any expertise or special knowledge [9,10]. Such method would be a valuable tool, especially in low-resource areas.

For point-of-care applications, which typically require a smaller degree of multiplexing, absorption-based approaches are attractive as they are more easily integrated with the analytes [11–13]. Hydrogel substrates are promising candidates as they have tunable pore sizes for the absorption of fluids [14,15]. Several recent reports described the use of hydrogel materials for biosensing [16–18]. Among the hydrogel materials, the nanoparticle incorporated hybrid conductive hydrogels are sensitive to a variety of stimuli, such as temperature, redox, pH, light, pressure, and strain, and their change can be easily detected by a distinct electronic signal [19–22]. However, only few reports focused on the POCT of the hydrogel in the cancer microenvironment, which are responsive to reactive oxygen species (ROS), extracellular pH and glutathione (GSH) [23,24]. Owing to the versatility of the hydrogel, the design of a conductive hydrogel-based sensor that responds to circumstances in cancer cell microenvironments might be used in cancer diagnosis. Specifically, despite their high sensitivity towards tiny physiological conditions, the application of pressure-strain sensing on hydrogel for detecting tumor microenvironment has not been explored, which is potential for cancer point-of-care diagnostic such as for such as for circulating tumor cell (CTC) and monitoring of tumor progression during surgery instead of biopsy [25,26]. Thus, it is interesting to develop the hydrogel-based sensor that capable to detect cancer microenvironment based on the pressure-strain responses, which can be easily operated without any specialized techniques and equipment. However, the stimuli-responsive nanoparticles, which will be incorporated into the hydrogel, must also be evaluated [27,28]. Carbon dots (CDs) have received a lot of interest among stimuli-responsive nanoparticles because of their appealing features such as fluorescence, biocompatibility, conductivity, and tunable surface property [29–33]. For example, diselenide-bridged CDs achieve ROS responsiveness owing to the breakdown of diselenide crosslinking under high ROS conditions [24,34]. Integrating ROS-sensitive CDs into conductive hydrogel sensors could be an effective strategy for the detection of the cancer microenvironment, which can be accomplished in the presence of cancer cells and normal cells by unique strain and pressure responses.

In this work, a cancer-microenvironment-selective conductive hydrogel sensor based on a ROS-responsive diselenide carbon dot hydrogel (dsCD-Hydrogel) has been developed, which demonstrated unique electronic signals for the applied pressure and strain in the presence of cancer cells and normal cells. The high ROS-containing tumor microenvironment causes the breaking of the diselenide bond in the presence of ROS, giving this sensor cancer selectivity. The presence of cancer cells can be identified by monitoring the pressure and strain sensing responses of the cancer cell-treated dsCD-Hydrogel, which was more sensitive than normal cell-treated dsCD-Hydrogel. In addition, the dsCD-Hydrogel may be used with a wireless sensing device to monitor the hydrogel's sensing capability in tumor-bearing mice, and in situ and ex-situ sensing data could be collected using a smartphone. The ROS scavenging activity and photothermal effect of dsCD were also found to decrease the tumor size by downregulating vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF-1α), and inducing tumor cell ablation, respectively.

Dopamine hydrochloride, triethylamine, selenium, sodium borohydride, sodium alginate (Alg, MW:150,000 g/mol), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), sodium chloride, ethanol, and hexane, were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (Yongin-si, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). penicillin–streptomycin, minimum essential medium (MEM), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) − 1640 medium, and Trypsin-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (0.05 % w/v trypsin-EDTA 1X), were bought from Gibco BRL (NY, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), Annexin V-FITC, SYTO-9, propidium iodide (PI), and 10X binding buffer, were obtained from Life Technologies (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 7.4) was obtained from Bioneer Corp. (Daejeon, Korea). The MDCK mammalian epithelial and HeLa cancer cell lines were obtained from the Korean Cell Line Bank (Seoul, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of diselenide-crosslinked carbon dot hydrogel (dsCD-Hydrogel)
ce:section.ce:para: The dsCD nanoparticle was first synthesized according to a previously published method [24]. To prepare the dsCD-Hydrogel, 50 mg dsCD was dispersed in 10 mL distilled water for 1 h and then stirred for 30 min. After the dsCD dispersion was obtained, 200 mg Alg was added, and the mixture was agitated for 2 h. until a uniform solution was achieved. Thereafter, 1 g of PVA was added and mixed at 90 °C completely dissolved. The hot and viscous solution was then casted on the mold. After cooling to room temperature, three freeze–thaw cycles were performed at −50 °C for 18 h and room temperature for 6 h. One reference hydrogel was prepared by the same method without the addition of dsCD and named PVA/Alginate (PVA/Alg)-Hydrogel.

During the mimicking of the ROS microenvironment, the hydrogels were treated with a 0.1 mM H2O2 solution. Essentially, the hydrogels were dipped into the 0.1 mM H2O2 solution for the treatment process, and were then removed and used for characterization.

The sensing performance of the dsCD-Hydrogel was evaluated by incubating with HeLa as a representative cancer cell and MDCK as a normal cell. Here, 1 mL of cell solution (105 cells/mL) was seeded into the dsCD-Hydrogel and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator. The cell-treated dsCD-Hydrogel was then rinsed three times with PBS (pH 7.4), and then subjected to conductivity measurement and pressure/strain sensing tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live and dead assay using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) measurement of the dsCD-Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Cells detached from the hydrogels were centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 3 min. The supernatant was removed and calcein AM (100 μL, live/green) was added and incubated for 15 min. Thereafter, propidium iodide (PI; dead/red) was added, and the cells were observed using CLSM.

MDCK and HeLa cells (105 cells/mL) were incubated depend on time in 96-wells plate containing dsCD-Hydrogel at 37 °C with a humidified 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Subsequently, the media and dsCD-Hydrogel were removed, followed by the addition of 20 µL MTT solution (1 mg/mL) and 180 µL fresh media, and subjected to another 4-h incubation. The media were removed and the MTT solubilization solution (200 µL) was then added and shaken for 15 min before the absorbances were measured at wavelength of 570 nm. Relative cell viability was calculated and compared to that of control cells. The cultured cells (MDCK and HeLa) only were used as a negative control, while the cells treated with toxic substance (DMSO 10 %) were used as a positive control.

Using a flow cytometer, the shifted fluorescence intensity was compared to the fluorescence of the cell only (control). The cells were labeled with Annexin V-FITC (living cells) and PI (dead cells) for apoptosis/necrosis analysis using a flow cytometer (excitation at 488 nm for both stains, emission at 530/30 and 574/26 nm, respectively).

The in vivo experiment conducted with female athymic mouse (4-week-old, 20–25) g body weight; Orient, Seoul, Korea). The anesthesia of the mice was performed using 200 µL xylazine (20 mg/kg) and ketamine (100 mg/kg). HeLa cells were injected subcutaneously (107 cells/injection), in the dorsal area of mice to produce tumors. PVA/Alg and dsCD-Hydrogels (4 × 4 × 2 mm) were put into the tumor three weeks after HeLa cells were subcutaneously injected into mice and treated with NIR laser (1.75 W/cm2 for 10 min). To test for inflammation, dsCD-Hydrogels were transplanted into the intramuscular sites of BALB/c mice. All animals received care according to the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals at Sungkyunkwan University (approval number: SKKUIACUC2021-09-08-1). A modified ellipsoidal method was used to compute tumor volume based on caliper readings. Tumor volume = π/6 × (length × width × height).
The ROS levels were determined using dihydroethidium (Invitrogen). Optimal cutting temperature (OCT) compound was used to implant cancer tissue samples (SciGen Scientific, Gardena, CA, USA).

Cancer tissue samples were embedded in optimum cutting temperature (OCT) compound (SciGen Scientific, Gardena, CA, USA). Specimens were cut into 10 µm slices, treated with dihydroethidium for 30 min, and stained with DAPI. Finally, a fluorescent microscope was used to examine the slices (DFC 3000 G; Leica, Wetzlar, Germany).

qRT-PCR was used to look at the relative gene expression levels of VEGF, HIF-1α, IL-1β, and IL-12. Total RNA was extracted from the samples using TRIzol reagent (1 mL: Life Technologies, Inc., CA, USA) and chloroform (200 µL). The lysates were centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm at 4 °C. The RNA pellets were washed in water with 75 % (v/v) ethanol, dried, and dissolved in RNase-free water. Following the manufacturer's instructions, qRT-PCR analysis was conducted using the SoAdvancedTM Universal SYBR Green Supermix kit (Bio-Rad, CA, USA) and the CFX ConnectTM real-time PCR detection system (Bio-Rad). Primer-BLAST was used to create all of the primers for qRT-PCR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo electrochemical performance by the ex-situ method of the dsCD-Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: After 3 days, the hydrogel implant was removed, and ex-situ measurement of the implanted dsCD-Hydrogel was conducted. Resistance was measured using EIS, a source meter, and a wireless sensing system. Pressure sensing was observed using an LCR and wireless sensing systems.

A cancer-microenvironment-selective pressure and strain-sensitive hydrogel was fabricated by incorporating ROS-responsive dsCD into a hydrogel matrix to target cancer cells via the monitoring of ROS levels. The hydrogel was fabricated by incorporating dsCD into a PVA-Alg [Poly(vinyl alcohol) and sodium alginate] matrix to form a dsCD-embedded double network hydrogel. In the high ROS concentration of cancer cells, the diselenide bond was broken and the particle size becomes smaller which increased the fluorescence intensity. The resulting particles also enhanced the charge carrier density. Accordingly, a difference in conductivity and pressure-strain response was found between normal and cancer cell conditions and was further detected by wireless sensing. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment was used to examine the ROS-induced changes in particle size, and the findings are displayed in Fig. S1a. Under normal conditions, the average diameter of dsCD was 418.58 nm (PDI = 0.39); however, after treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2 for 12 h, the diameter decreased to 98.24 nm (PDI = 0.33). The breaking of the Se–Se link caused the particle size to decrease, which was triggered by high ROS concentration [24,34]. The UV–vis-NIR spectroscopy of dsCD was also performed before and after H2O2 treatment. The spectra showed the different absorption in NIR region (greater than800 nm) which differentiate the photothermal generation between before and after H2O2 treatment (Fig. S1b). To study the fluorescence behavior of dsCD, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed at different H2O2 concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 10 mM (Fig. S1c). Fig. 1 b, exhibited that the PL intensity of dsCD after treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2 was higher than that of the non-treated dsCD due to the breaking of the diselenide bond, which increased the number of particles and lowered the possibility of aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon in CD, ultimately resulting in fluorescence recovery [24,34]. During the optimization of treatment time, a time-dependent increase in the fluorescence intensity was observed; however, after 12 h of treatment time fluorescence intensity did not increase significantly (Fig. S1d). Based on this observation, the treatment time was confined to 12 h. The change in the fluorescence behavior of dsCD was retained when incorporated into the hydrogel matrix, and was further confirmed by the confocal microscopic image and the corresponding depth profiles of the hydrogel (Fig. 1c, S2). The dsCD-Hydrogel was then characterized to evaluate the swelling behavior and degradation profile. The swelling ratio of dsCD-Hydrogel was found to be 744 % (Fig. S3a), with no degradation was found as indicated by 100 % retained mass after 4 days (Fig. S3b). The release of dsCD nanoparticle from hydrogel was also assessed under different biological conditions, with pH 6.8 and H2O2 solutions were used as representatives of cancer microenvironment and pH 7.4 was used as normal biological condition (Fig. S4). By comparing the absorbance of solution before and after treatment, the release of dsCD nanoparticle was negligible (∼0.10 %) for those biological conditions, indicating stability of dsCD-Hydrogel. The effect of ROS on the morphology of the hydrogels was examined by SEM (Fig. 1d). The pore size of the hydrogel was found to increase after treatment, which affected the mechanical behavior of the hydrogel [35,36]. Based on Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) analysis using CO2-adsorption isotherm, the pore sizes of dsCD-Hydrogel before and after H2O2 treatment were 59.27 m2/g and 76.36 m2/g, respectively, confirming the effect of ROS on hydrogel porosity (Fig. S5). The ROS scavenging properties of the dsCD-Hydrogel were determined based on the inhibitory effect of the hydrogels on diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radicals (Fig. 1e). The introduction of dsCD was found to significantly enhance the DPPH scavenging capacity of dsCD-Hydrogels to 63 %. Furthermore, the mechanical properties of the hydrogels were evaluated by tensile measurements under tumor microenvironment conditions including H2O2 and acidic pH (pH 6.8). The dsCD-Hydrogel without any treatment had a stretchability of 485 % with Young’s modulus of 35.3 kPa, which similar with that hydrogel treated in pH 6.8 (Young’s modulus = 34.1 kPa). However, after 12 h of treatment with H2O2, the stretchability increased to 573 % and mechanical strength decreased, with Young’s modulus of 5.6 kPa (Fig. 1f). This finding indicates that the hydrogel became softer after the breakage of the diselenide bond, and the extended pore size increased the stretchability and flexibility of the resultant hydrogel. Dynamic strain sweeps were used to estimate the linear viscoelastic (LVE) range, which is generally referred to as the plateau of G′ and G″ [37]. The apparent LVE range of the H2O2-treated hydrogel was greater than that of the pristine hydrogel and hydrogel treated in pH 6.8 (Fig. 1g), suggesting that the H2O2-treated hydrogel is more elastic and stable when subjected to oscillating strain. The drop of the onset in G′ and raise in G″ implies mutilation to the hydrogel's internal framework [38]. As a result, for the ensuing dynamic frequency sweep studies, a strain value of 50 % (within the LVE range) was used (Fig. S6). G′ was generally steady and greater than G″ over the tested frequency range (0.1–100 Hz). Such findings indicate that both dsCD-Hydrogel and H2O2-treated dsCD-Hydrogel, exhibited and retained the characteristic gel phase from low to high shear frequencies. The extra physical crosslinking (i.e., H-bonds between cleaved Se-O and –OH of polymer chains), promote the enhancement of LVE [39]. The rheological study revealed that the H2O2 treated dsCD-Hydrogel contained more physical crosslinks than the dsCD-Hydrogel when subjected to dynamic external stresses. Even though the higher physical crosslinks, the strength recovery is virtually reversible within 100 s, as verified by structural recovery tests under alternate stresses of 0.1 percent and 50 %. (Fig. 1h). The enhanced stretchability and softness can be inferred from the FTIR spectra (Fig. S7a) of the lyophilized hydrogels. Hydrogen bond formation between the cleaved carbon dot, PVA, and Alg leads to a more stretchable and reversible hydrogel for the regain of physical strength. The increased hydrogen bonding density was further confirmed by the DSC measurements, where the glass transition temperature (Tg) enhanced from 72 °C to 78 °C, while the thermal stability of the hydrogel decreased, according to the decrease in the melting temperature (Tm) from 204 to 192 °C. The H2O2-treated samples showed a modest change in Tm toward a lower temperature, indicating the conversion from a semi-crystalline to an amorphous structure (Fig. S7b). Owing to a more flexible amorphous region trapped in or close to the crystalline matrix, the hydrogel may melt at lower temperatures [40]. Such notion implies the crystallites are suppressed thus there is an increase in the amorphous region within the polymer, which decreases the mechanical strength but increases the stretchability; this occurrence favors transport, ultimately enhancing the conductivity of the hydrogel.

To investigate the changes in the electronic behavior under ROS conditions, the conductivity of the synthesized dsCD-Hydrogel was evaluated. Based on the EIS curves, the resistance of the dsCD-Hydrogel after ROS treatment (10.8 kΩ) was considerably lesser than that without treatment (18.7 kΩ) (Fig. 2
a). To determine the reason for the enhancement of the conductivity, an H2O2 concentration-dependent (Fig. S8a) and treatment time-dependent (Fig. S8b) study was performed, which showed that after 12 h of treatment with 10 mM H2O2, the resistance did not significantly change. Accordingly, an H2O2 concentration of 10 mM and treatment time of 12 h were employed. The improvement in conductivity is due to the diselenide structure breaking down, resulting in smaller particles that help the electron transport procedure which shorten the percolation network in the dsCD-Hydrogel [24]. These tinier nanoparticles acquire higher surface charge, which accelerates the movement of charged species, enabling an increase in conductivity. This resistance trend was further confirmed by evaluating the resistance value from the sourcemeter, showing decrease of resistance as the treatment time increase (Fig. S8c). The change in the resistance behavior was also traced using a custom-made wireless resistance sensor (Fig. S8d). The display showed the decrease of resistance along with increase of H2O2 concentration. Moreover, the brightness of the 1.9 V white LED was enhanced with increased power consumption from 1.058 to 42.05 to mW owing to a decrease in resistance with increased H2O2 concentration (Fig. S8e). These wireless sensing and LED experiment further demonstrated the distinct conductivity between dsCD-Hydrogel before and after treatment with H2O2 (Fig. 2b). Due to the difference in physical strength, stretchability, and electronic behavior under different H2O2 conditions, a pressure and strain sensor was fabricated to detect abnormal ROS conditions. The distinct pressure sensitivity gauge factor (GF) of the dsCD-Hydrogel was analyzed by using regulated from a UTM followed by observing the resistance change (ΔR/R0). Fig. 2c shows the enhanced GF of 2.37 kPa−1 for the H2O2-treated hydrogel compared with the non-treated dsCD-Hydrogel (1.24 kPa−1). The current change signal was extremely reproducible, stable, and sensitive throughout a large compressive range of 5–20 kPa, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. Furthermore, the bending strain sensing stability and bending strain gauge factor were evaluated by affixing the hydrogel on the index finger and performing repeated stretching and bending. The rigid dsCD-Hydrogel was found to easily break after 1240 cycles of operation; however, after 12 h of H2O2 treatment, the strain-sensing responses were retained after 2000 cycles (Fig. 2e). The distinct bending strain sensitivity was also found between treated and non-treated dsCD-Hydrogel, indicated by bending strain GF value of 0.058 and 0.075 for dsCD-Hydrogel before and after H2O2 treatment, respectively (Fig. S9a). Because of the detecting ability and sensitivity of dsCD-Hydrogel sensor, the bending of the finger can be precisely recorded at various angles from 0° to 120°, with distinct response between dsCD-Hydrogel before and after H2O2 treatment (Fig. S9b). Moreover, GF value of tensile strain sensitivity was measured different between before and after H2O2 treatment. Similarly with pressure and bending strain sensing, dsCD-Hydrogel with H2O2 treatment demonstrated higher tensile sensitivity (GF = 0.056) compared to that without treatment (GF = 0.046) (Fig. S10). Fig. 2f further shows the relative resistance change (ΔR/R0) under stepping strain from 0 % to 150 % reversibly. This analysis proved that the hydrogel sensor consistently responded to a wide range of strains, even a minor strain where the detecting strain was too difficult, such as from 0 % to 25 %. This benefit establishes the groundwork for tracking minor to large-scale human movements. Due to the high strain sensitivity of the dsCD-Hydrogel after H2O2 treatment, it could sense wrist pulses (Fig. 2g). In the stationary state, 63 pulse signals per minute were identified by the dsCD-Hydrogel sensor after 12 h of H2O2 treatment; however, no prominent signal was observed for the dsCD-Hydrogel without treatment. As the hydrogel can detect deformation and bending, it can be used for writing pad application. Fig. S11a illustrates the design of the hydrogel writing pad for identifying handwriting response. To measure handwriting movement, the hydrogel was layered between two VHB films with electrodes on each side. As exhibited, the hydrogel could recognize simple letters (A, B, C, D, E, F) (Fig. 2h) and random complex letters (Fig. S11b), when the testers wrote on the hydrogel writing board, a radically different signal patterns have been observed. After H2O2 treatment, the response became more intense and distinguishable (Fig. 2i and Fig. S11c) owing to enhanced electromechanical performance. Thus, the ROS-selective directional sensing property can be utilized for electromechanical cancer biosensors.

To assess the ROS responsiveness of the dsCD-Hydrogel, the effect of cell detachment and nanoparticle absorption on the electronic characteristics of the dsCD-Hydrogel has been investigated. MDCK and HeLa cells were incubated with hydrogels at a concentration of 105 cells/mL for 24 h. Both hydrogels were then dipped in PBS pH 7.4 for 1 h to remove the cells. Confocal imaging and flow cytometry analysis (FACS) were used to validate the cellular uptake of dsCD. Confocal microscopic images exhibited intense fluorescence for HeLa cells and much decreased fluorescence intensity for MDCK cells, as seen in Fig. 3a. Such finding indicates that at low ROS concentrations, the dsCD particles remained in the aggregated state and showed ACQ, whereas at high ROS concentrations, the particles existed in a free state regaining the fluorescence intensity. The mean fluorescence intensity of HeLa cells shifted more than that of MDCK cells, according to FACS data, indicating that the ROS microenvironment created by the cancer cells removes the ACQ, frees the particles from aggregation, and turns the fluorescence to the “on” state; for normal cells (MDCK), the ACQ sustained the fluorescence “off” state (Fig. 3b). These findings showed that ROS triggered fluorescence “off” and “on” states in MDCK and HeLa cells, respectively, which influenced the mechanical and electrical characteristics of the dsCD-Hydrogel. The MDCK-treated dsCD-Hydrogel had a stretchability of 493 %, with a Young’s modulus of 26.1 kPa. In contrast, the HeLa-treated hydrogel had a stretchability of 535 %, a decrease in mechanical strength, and a Young’s modulus of 6.8 kPa (Fig. S12a). A similar trend was observed for the rheological frequency sweep (Fig. S12b) and strain sweep (Fig. S12c), measurement where the cancer microenvironment accelerates the decrease in the G′ and G″ values, and an increase in the LVE. To ensure the biocompatibility of dsCD-Hydrogel, MTT assay was conducted by incubating HeLa and MDCK cells depend on time (Fig. S13a, b). For this experiment, the cultured cells (HeLa or MDCK only) were used as a negative control which presented 100 % cell viability, and the cells treated with toxic substance (DMSO 10 %) were used as a positive control which was known decreasing the cell viability. Compared to the controls, the results suggested that dsCD-Hydrogel was biocompatible, indicated by almost 100 % cell viability after incubation for 24 h. Moreover, the confocal microscopy images in Fig. S13c revealed a trace amount of dead HeLa cells after cell detachment, which explains the excellent biocompatibility of the dsCD-Hydrogel. The changes in the physical properties of the hydrogel in the cancer microenvironment and the SEM morphology of the hydrogel were examined (Fig. S13d), which revealed the enlarged pore size of the hydrogel, accounting for the enhanced stretchability and softness of the HeLa-embedded dsCD-Hydrogel compared with the MDCK-embedded dsCD-Hydrogel. Strain reversibility is important for strain sensors. Under a cyclic tensile strain of 100 %, the stress enhanced as strain increased and could return to its original level, suggesting that the structure was not damaged in any way with such significant deformation (Fig. S14a). The energy of dissipation for the dsCD-Hydrogel (13.56 kJ/m2) and MDCK treated dsCD-Hydrogel (11.96 kJ/m2) was markedly higher than that of the H2O2-treated (0.87 kJ/m2) and HeLa-treated dsCD-Hydrogel (1.32 kJ/m2), which suggests the quick recovery of the hydrogel under large strain condition with high ROS concentration. To evaluate the change in conductivity of the hydrogel in the cancer microenvironment, the resistance was measured using EIS after detachment of the cells from the hydrogel (Fig. 3c). For the MDCK-treated dsCD-Hydrogel, the resistance value was 18.88 kΩ, which is similar to that of the pristine hydrogel. The HeLa-treated dsCD- hydrogel had a resistance of 11.92 kΩ, which mimics the similar ROS condition and establishes the effect of the cancer microenvironment on the dsCD-Hydrogel, where breakage of the diselenide structure enhanced the charge carrier density and the relatively smaller size conducting particles enhanced the mobility [9,24]. The evaluation of electrical and mechanical properties of dsCD-Hydrogel after treated with cancer cell (HeLa) and normal cell (MDCK) was conducted by removing the cells from the hydrogel, which the results were compared with dsCD-Hydrogel without any treatment as a control. Compared to the dsCD-Hydrogel without any treatment (Fig. 3c and S14a, label: dsCD-Hydrogel), the HeLa-treated dsCD-Hydrogel showed distinct electrical and mechanical properties after removing the cancer cell and tumor condition. In contrast, the electrical and mechanical properties of MDCK-treated dsCD-Hydrogel were similar to the dsCD-Hydrogel without any treatment. This result showed that the HeLa-treated dsCD-Hydrogel was not able to recover their electrical and mechanical properties because the cleaved diselenide bond in dsCD cannot reformed to its initial structure.

The trend in the change of resistance was further confirmed by measuring the resistance value using the source meter (Fig. S14b) and wireless responses (Fig. S14c). Based on these findings, we investigated the pressure sensing response of the cell-treated dsCD-Hydrogel after 12 h of incubation using HeLa and MDCK cells. The pressure response of the HeLa-treated dsCD-Hydrogel was significantly higher than that of the MDCK-treated dsCD-Hydrogel at 5, 10, and 20 kPa, as shown in Fig. 3d. Alternatively, sensing at a pressure of 20 kPa was recorded by the wireless sensor for real-time detection, exhibiting a similar trend (Fig. S14d). The enhanced pressure sensitivity was associated with the directional pressure sensing of the hydrogel. Accordingly, the dsCD-Hydrogel presented a distinguishable response for writing the characters on the dsCD-Hydrogel surface with high intensity for HeLa-treated dsCD-Hydrogel (Fig. 3f) compared to MDCK-treated dsCD-Hydrogel (Fig. 3e). Furthermore, without treatment with the ROS microenvironment, the hydrogel cannot distinguish the resistance response of complex characters, such as K, N, U, and T, owing to a lack of sensitivity (Fig. S15). The dsCD-Hydrogel, when exposed to the cancer microenvironment, the H2O2 present in the ROS atmosphere catalyzed the diselenide bond breakage, which led to the generation of oxidized species from selenide and Se–O, resulting in a decrease in the overall ROS concentration and ROS scavenging activity. To evaluate the ROS scavenging activity, an H2O2 assay was used to determine the H2O2 level, which showed a 13.9 % decrease in the H2O2 concentration at 12 h after embedding HeLa cells (Fig. 3g). Fig. S16 shows the excellent DPPH inhibition of 35.4 % by the dsCD-Hydrogel after 12 h of incubation of the HeLa cells, further confirming the radical scavenging activity of the designed system. The dsCD-Hydrogel was endowed with NIR photothermal heat generation, which was dependent on the thickness of the hydrogel (Fig. S17a). A maximum heat generation of 35 °C was achieved by the 0.1 cm thick hydrogel after treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2 (Fig. S17b). The penetration of the NIR laser was effective for the thinner hydrogel of 0.1 cm; as the hydrogel became thicker than 0.2 cm, the NIR laser could not penetrate, leading to a decrease in photothermal heat generation. For practical application purposes, handling of the 0.1 cm thick hydrogel is difficult. Accordingly, a thickness of 0.2 cm was selected to evaluate the photothermal application of the hydrogel where a maximum heat generation of 27 °C was achieved. The photothermal heat generation was also assessed under multiple NIR irradiations using different laser power (0.5–2 W/cm2), which both dsCD-Hydrogels (before and after H2O2 treatment) showed higher photothermal heat generation when higher laser power was used (Fig. S17c, d). Furthermore, each dsCD-Hydrogels possessed distinct photothermal conversion efficiency (η), with η of hydrogel after H2O2 treatment was higher (34.88 %) than that hydrogel before treatment (18.96 %) (Fig. S18). After examining photothermal heat generation it was used to successfully eradicate cancer cells in vitro, we looked at cell survival after 5 min of NIR irradiation. HeLa cells were initially incubated on dsCD-Hydrogel samples for 24 h, and then being exposed to NIR light. The cells were removed after PBS washing, and the killing impact of photothermal therapy (PTT) was assessed utilizing the live and dead cell assay and FACS to confirm the cell viability, which revealed almost 100 % live cells without NIR treatment (Fig. 3h). In contrast, more than 90 % of the cells died after 7 min of NIR irradiation owing to necrosis induced by the photothermal heat (Fig. 3i). The photothermal treatment of HeLa cells was investigated using live and dead cell assays and confocal imaging, which revealed red fluorescence toward a small number of green signals, proving the successful killing of cancer cells by photothermal treatment (Fig. 3j). The combined effect of ROS-scavenging activity and NIR ablation of tumor cells provides compelling evidence for PTT.

Our system is focused on the sensing of tumor and normal microenvironment conditions based on ROS behavior in tumor, which the overproduction of ROS is not only related with tumor, but also with ROS-related diseases, such as vascular injury and inflammatory diseases [47,48]. Because of excellent ROS scavenging ability, it is also possible that dsCD-Hydrogel to be applied in such diseases. Our system can detect ROS in tumor by implanting dsCD-Hydrogel directly into the tumor site, which further scavenges the ROS and generates photothermal heat under NIR irradiation for theragnosis purposes. A tumor-bearing mouse model (back of athymic nude mice) was utilized to investigate the sensing performance and photothermal efficacy of the dsCD-Hydrogel. Three days after implantation, the conductivity of the implanted hydrogel in mice with cancer and normal mice was monitored ex-situ using a wireless sensing system (Fig. 4 a). Simultaneously, NIR treatment was performed for 10 min each day (1.75 W/cm2, 808 nm). Owing to NIR irradiation, the temperature of the dsCD-Hydrogel containing tumor-bearing sites increased by 12.3 °C and reached 43.1 °C, which is markedly sufficient for photothermal ablation of cancer cells. In contrast, PVA/Alg-Hydrogel did not show any significant increase in temperature and reached only 32.3 °C (Fig. 4b). The ROS scavenging activity of the dsCD-Hydrogel helped to decrease the tumor size and NIR treatment accelerated the ablation of the solid tumor, as shown in Fig. 4c. The decrease in tumor size was verified by immunofluorescence staining, which revealed the variation in tumor hypoxia by vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF-1α) expression (Fig. 4d). As quantitively analyzed, the dsCD-Hydrogel with NIR treatment induced a 53.2 % and 48.4 % decrease in the relative value of VEGF and HIF-1α expression, respectively. HIF-1α, which is generated by tumor cells, plays an important role in the adaptive response to hypoxia by influencing a variety of cellular activities including proliferation, apoptosis, angiogenesis, and anaerobic glycolysis [41,42]. HIF-1α binds to the hypoxia-responsive element after activation, boosting the transcription of a variety of genes, including VEGF. The expression of VEGF promotes angiogenesis and contributes to the formation of malignant tumors [43,44]. As a result, reducing tumor hypoxia would prevent tumor growth by downregulating HIF-1α and VEGF production, which is indeed beneficial for promoting dsCD-mediated synergistic therapeutic performance in vivo. The ROS-scavenging activity at the tumor site of the implanted hydrogel was evaluated by ROS staining using a dihydroethidium assay kit (Fig. 4e). The confocal microscope image revealed the presence of a high amount of ROS at the tumor site for the PVA/Alg-Hydrogel; however, when dsCD-Hydrogel was implanted, there was a significant reduction in the ROS level due to the ROS scavenging activity of the hydrogel. Interestingly, when NIR treatment was performed on the dsCD-Hydrogel, the ROS level almost reached the minimal state, which can be attributed to the ablation of cancer cells due to photothermal heat generation. Fig. S19a shows an image of the implanted hydrogel in a normal mouse to check for inflammation. After insertion, the dsCD-Hydrogel did not show any serious inflammation in the surrounding tissue (Fig. S19b). To further characterize allergic inflammation in the skin of a normal mouse, the levels of pro-inflammatory (M1) tissue cytokines at the site of the dsCD-Hydrogel insertion were determined, and the results were compared with those of tissue samples from normal mice without dsCD-Hydrogel insertion. The pro-inflammatory cytokines, interleukin-1β (IL-1β) and interleukin-12 (IL-12), are responsible for periodontal tissue destruction and inflammation.[45,46] The expression of IL-1β (Fig. S19c) and IL-12 (Fig. S19d) did not increase significantly after dsCD-Hydrogel implantation, which exhibited noninflammatory characteristics of the dsCD-Hydrogel. Moreover, the non-inflammatory characteristics accelerated PTT-induced tumor ablation. The selectivity of the dsCD-Hydrogel toward cancer cells was monitored by electrochemical detection. EIS and a source meter were used to verify the conductivities of the implanted dsCD-Hydrogels ex-situ. The EIS measurements demonstrated a decrease in the conductivity of the dsCD-Hydrogel after 3 days of implantation in cancer mice. Compared with the dsCD-Hydrogel implantation in normal mice (control), the resistance of the hydrogel implanted in tumor-bearing animals decreased significantly, but the resistance of the hydrogel made without dsCD (i.e., PVA/Alg hydrogel (control)) changed just little (Fig. S20a). The source meter resistance also revealed that for hydrogels implanted in normal mouse, the PVA/Alg hydrogel showed high resistance (Fig. S20b) and low-pressure sensing response based on the LCR (Fig. S20c) and wireless measurements (Fig. S20d) compared with the dsCD-Hydrogel; this is obvious because conducting dsCD in the dsCD-Hydrogel makes it more conductive than the pristine PVA/Alg hydrogel even under normal ROS conditions. Moreover, in tumor-bearing mice, the resistance of the PVA/Alg hydrogel remained unchanged (Fig. 4f); however, the resistance value of the dsCD-Hydrogel was markedly reduced (Fig. S20e), indicating that the resistance value is also consistent with the high internal ROS concentration in the tumor microenvironment. Furthermore, the dsCD-Hydrogel implanted in tumor-bearing mice respond to pressure more sensitively than the PVA/Alg-Hydrogel (Fig. 4g, S20f). After collecting the implanted hydrogel, a wireless sensor device was employed to monitor pressure sensing. The use of wireless system on monitoring the responses from implanted hydrogel was aimed to qualitatively provide a simple and rapid observation of ROS or cancer detection in point-of-care diagnostics. In tumor-bearing mice, the pressure sensitivity of the dsCD-Hydrogel was higher than that of the control hydrogel and the dsCD-Hydrogel implanted in normal mice (Fig. 4h, S20g). As a result, the dsCD-Hydrogel has been shown to be sensitive to the tumor microenvironment and to function well in vivo in discriminating cancers from normal tissue. Furthermore, the PTT did not significantly affect the conductivity; however, the sensing response of the dsCD-Hydrogel slightly deteriorated (Fig. 4f–h) as the movement of the particles may be restricted due to the imposed rigidity of the network structure after PTT. Thus, not only is this tumor microenvironment-selective touch sensor biocompatible, but it is also efficient and sensitive for pressure-strain-based tumor identification.

This work effectively developed a mechanically and electrochemically controllable cancer microenvironment-sensitive touch sensor with the abolition of solid tumors based on ROS-responsive CD-embedded conductive hydrogels. The ROS condition of the cancer microenvironment cleaved the diselenide bond, which produced smaller particles with enhanced mobility and charge carriers; showing tunable conductivity and fluorescence on/off behavior, which is the driving force for the detection of the cancer environment. The dsCD-Hydrogel displayed 122 % enhanced stretchability, 52 % increased conductivity, and 91 % improved pressure/strain sensitivity, as revealed by the handwriting response toward cancer cells treated dsCD-Hydrogel (HeLa) compared to normal cells (MDCK). The in vivo photothermal temperature reached 43.1 °C, which aided in the ablation of cancer cells. The ROS scavenging activity of dsCD-Hydrogel with NIR treatment showed a 53.2 % and 48.4 % decrease in the causative factors of tumor hypoxia, such as HIF-1α and VEGF expression, respectively, which inhibited tumor progression and displayed synergistic therapeutic performance. The inflammatory expression of IL-1β and IL-12 did not increase significantly and showed non-inflammatory characteristics of the dsCD-Hydrogel. Furthermore, in vivo investigations in tumor-bearing mice indicated that the implanted dsCD-Hydrogel displayed superior tumor sensing compared to normal tissue. A wireless sensor device was used to provide real-time conductivity and strain–pressure profiles on a smartphone to ease the monitoring procedure. Thus, this tumor-specific strain–pressure responding hydrogel-based sensor provides a simple method for detecting tumors that is both sensitive and selective, and it has the potential to be used in POCT.
